title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: Year-End Trading Surge Followed by Price Declines as Amended Regulations Take Effect in the CA LCFS Market
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights: (30th December â€“ 5th January)
  • A total of 311,250 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a fall of 84.40% week-over-week (WoW), and all non-zero trades decreased by 53.30% WoW as 1,126,175 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price decreased this week to $74.07/MTÂ for Type 1Â transfers, recording a 2.76%Â fall WoW; All Non-Zero transfers fell by 6.84% in price, reaching $70.86/MT.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price decreased by 0.37% WoW; On 7th Jan, the spot price stood at $74.88/MT. The benchmark price stood at $81.50/MT on 7th Jan.D3 RIN prices increased by 3.42% WoW and D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices increase by 1.72% WoW.
cCarbonâ€™s Market View The final week of December witnessed significant trading activity as companies worked to close balances ahead of the year-end. From the first week of January 2025, the amended regulation by CARB will come into effect, and its ability to stabilize prices and prevent significant declines will become clearer by the end of the first quarter. For an in-depth analysis of trading activity and price dynamics in the California LCFS market, refer to our Month-in-Review report. As California begins 2025 with the adoption of a more ambitious Low Carbon Fuel Standard (LCFS), the year will also see the implementation of stricter targets under the Advanced Clean Trucks (ACT) and Advanced Clean Fleets (ACF) regulations. These initiatives aim to reduce emissions in the hard-to-decarbonize medium and heavy-duty vehicle (MHDV) sector by addressing both demand and supply. Together, these complementary regulations are designed to accelerate the transition to zero-emission vehicles (ZEVs). While ZEVs now represent approximately 30% of Class 2b sales, Classes 3-8 remain well below the 2024 ACT targets, requiring substantial growth, particularly in Classes 4-8 and 7-8. The expansion of charging infrastructure, including 10,548 DC Fast Charging outlets, has supported this transition. For further insights, watch our webinar here.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

[caption id="attachment_591009" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 prices recorded a 2.76% WoW fall, decreasing from $76.17 to $74.07 this week, with all non-zero credit prices decreased by 6.84% WoW, falling from $76.06 to $70.86. Type 1 trades saw 311,250 credits exchanged, reflecting a 84.40% WoW decrease. Similarly, the total transaction volume for all non-zero trades declined by 53.30% WoW to reach 1,126,175 credits.

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

[caption id="attachment_591013" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices decreased marginally by 0.37% WoW, reaching $75.44/MT. On 7th January, the average spot price was $74.88/MT, while the benchmark, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2025, stood at $81.50/MT as of 7th January.

CA LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

[caption id="attachment_591012" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, 83 trades were reported in the market, with ten high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was $80.00 for Type 2 transactions with a volume of 5,000 credits. Last week, the lowest transfer by price was $47.50 per credit of Type 2, reported for two transactions at volumes of 5,000 and 10,000 credits. The smallest volume transfers of 2,000 credits of Type 2 category, were undertaken for price of $76.50 whereas the largest volume of transfer was reported at 80,000 MT of Type 1 category at $75.25.

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_591011" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] In November 2024, trading volumes stood at 2.01M, which increased significantly to 7.38M in December 2024, representing a 2.67-fold rise. This substantial growth in trading activity is attributed to year-end clearing of balances by companies. Comparatively, trading volumes in December 2023 were recorded at 6.09M. Regarding prices, the average Type 1 prices in November 2024 were $73.07, slightly higher than December 2024, where the average price was $71.89. Despite this minor decline, prices remain on a positive trajectory overall, driven by the approval of amendments in November 2024.

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_591010" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 30th Dec â€“ 5th Jan, total VPT recorded a 14.81% decrease (WoW), with all non-zero prices falling over the previous week.

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN weekly price trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices increased by 3.42% WoW and D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices increase by 1.72% WoW. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming dates

Key Clean Fuels Market Developments this week

U.S. Department of the Treasury Releases Final Rules for Clean Hydrogen Production Tax Credit. The U.S. Treasury and IRS have finalized Section 45V Clean Hydrogen Tax Credit rules, ensuring clarity, flexibility, and investment certainty while emphasizing on GHG limits to remain below 4 kg COâ‚‚e per kg. The rules extend annual time-matching by two years, mandate hourly matching by 2030, and expand incrementality pathways for nuclear, CCS, and state-capped power. For methane-based hydrogen, they refine methane leakage assessments, broaden RNG sources, and introduce a book-and-claim system by 2027. Projects can lock in 45VH2-GREET models at construction, fostering clean hydrogen growth and U.S. leadership in the sector.
